info: The Abandoned Shipwreck Act of 1987 (ASA) was enacted following the Submerged Lands Act of 1953, with the intention of further protecting shipwrecks in the rivers and lakes of the United States as well as any shipwrecks up to three miles offshore into the ocean. Under this legislation, any shipwrecks deemed “abandoned” on state land are owned by the state, federal lands by the federal government, and tribal lands by the associated tribe—the only major exception to this is that of U.S. Army and Foreign Military vessel wrecks, which continue to be owned by the respective government no matter where they are located.

The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) identified that Kalamazoo County/Kalamazoo Battle Creek International Airport (AZO) requires a significant expansion of runway 17-35 to keep up with operational and safety demands of the airport. A Section 4(f) evaluation was conducted by Mead & Hunt and reviewed by the US Department of the Interior. The evaluation found that Bloomfield Subdivision Historic District, a NRHP eligible post-WW2 community, would be adversely affected.

On October 15, 1966, the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) was signed into law by President Lyndon B. Johnson. Prompted by public and professional anxieties about the destruction faced by historically significant places during Post-WWII development and expansion, the NHPA functioned as a mandate for 1) a national preservation program and 2) a system of protections to encourage the identification and protection of historic resources (National Park Service 2025a).
